candidates.foreshadow_summary: Mathilde finds a superb diamond necklace in a black satin box at Madame Forestier's, her heart throbs with immoderate desire, and she puts it on in ecstasy.
candidates.payoff_summary: Madame Forestier reveals that the original necklace was paste and worth only five hundred francs.

proposed_trigger.description: Mathilde loses the necklace after the ball and is forced to replace it with a genuine diamond necklace at great cost.
observable_conditions: Mathilde discovers the necklace is missing (P0059)
They find a replacement necklace for 36,000 francs and buy it (P0086-P0089).

candidates.relation_description: The necklace's assumed value is unchallenged when borrowed, creating a hidden truth that is only exposed at the end, rendering the decade of sacrifice unnecessary.
candidates.stage1_rationale: This is the central twist of the story, fitting the object-foreshadow pattern perfectly.

candidates.foreshadow_summary: Mathilde is deeply unhappy with her modest circumstances, constantly dreaming of luxury and elegance, and feeling she was born for a higher station.
candidates.payoff_summary: After ten years of poverty, Mathilde has become a strong, rough woman with frowsy hair and red hands, who only occasionally remembers the ball where she was beautiful.

proposed_trigger.description: The loss of the necklace forces the Loisels into a decade of harsh toil to repay the replacement debt.
observable_conditions: They borrow huge sums and buy a replacement (P0088-P0089)
They move to a garret and perform manual labor (P0093-P0097).

candidates.relation_description: Her early psychological dissatisfaction with her social status ironically leads to a life of even greater hardship, a physical transformation that embodies the very poverty she once despised.
candidates.stage1_rationale: Although this is a character trait rather than a concrete object, it functions as a motivational foreshadow that is paid off with the decay of her beauty and status.

candidates.foreshadow_summary: Madame Forestier generously and casually lends Mathilde any jewel she wants, including the diamond necklace, without hesitation.
candidates.payoff_summary: Madame Forestier reveals the necklace was paste, suggesting she was never worried about the loan because of its low value.

proposed_trigger.description: Madame Forestier reacts indifferently when the necklace is returned, not even inspecting it.
observable_conditions: Madame Forestier says 'You should have returned it sooner' but does not open the case (P0091-P0092).

candidates.relation_description: Her nonchalant lending and later failure to check the returned item retroactively hint at the necklace's worthlessness.
candidates.stage1_rationale: This is a subtle behavioral clue that only becomes meaningful after the twist reveal; less central than the necklace object itself.
